subtopic_detail: Second-trimester medical termination after foetal anomaly
stem: You are the obstetrics registrar in a tertiary maternal-foetal medicine clinic in New South Wales seeing a 34-year-old non-Aboriginal woman at 21+0 weeks gestation with a recently confirmed lethal foetal anomaly diagnosed on the morphology scan and reconfirmed by tertiary review. She and her partner request termination of pregnancy. She has had one previous uncomplicated vaginal birth. She has no medical comorbidity, no allergies and is Rh-positive. She has been counselled by the genetic counsellor and social worker.

data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender uterus consistent with dates, cervix long and closed on speculum
data_table.Investigations: Detailed morphology and tertiary review confirm lethal anomaly, FBC normal, group and hold sent, MSU clear, infection screen negative

lead_in: What is the single best management plan?

options.text: Commence vaginal misoprostol 200 micrograms every 6 hours alone without mifepristone priming and admit to the antenatal ward with intermittent CTG monitoring throughout the process
options.rationale: Mifepristone priming significantly shortens induction-to-delivery time at this gestation; misoprostol alone is less effective and CTG monitoring after a lethal diagnosis adds no value. (Source: RANZCOG Termination of Pregnancy Statement)
source_refs: RANZCOG Termination of Pregnancy Statement

options.text: Arrange outpatient surgical dilatation and evacuation under conscious sedation in the next 48 hours, without antenatal cervical preparation, and discharge home after recovery
options.rationale: Surgical D&E at 21 weeks requires cervical preparation with mifepristone or osmotic dilators and access to a surgeon trained in second-trimester procedures; outpatient sedation without preparation is unsafe. (Source: RANZCOG Termination of Pregnancy Statement)
source_refs: RANZCOG Termination of Pregnancy Statement

options.text: Admit for combined regimen: oral mifepristone 200 mg followed in 24 to 48 hours by vaginal or buccal misoprostol 400 micrograms every 3 hours, with multidisciplinary support and bereavement care
options.rationale: Australian second-trimester medical termination uses mifepristone priming followed by misoprostol; combined regimens shorten induction time and reduce blood loss. Bereavement care and lactation suppression counselling are core components. (Source: RANZCOG Termination of Pregnancy Statement)
source_refs: RANZCOG Termination of Pregnancy Statement
Royal Women's Hospital Termination of Pregnancy Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Arrange oxytocin infusion alone to induce labour in the antenatal ward and plan vaginal birth with continuous foetal heart monitoring throughout the procedure
options.rationale: Oxytocin alone is rarely effective for cervical priming at 21 weeks; the standard regimen is mifepristone plus misoprostol, with oxytocin reserved for retained placenta. CTG monitoring is not appropriate after lethal diagnosis. (Source: RANZCOG Termination of Pregnancy Statement)
source_refs: RANZCOG Termination of Pregnancy Statement

options.text: Defer the procedure and recommend continuation of pregnancy with palliative care planning for the newborn, citing late gestation as an obstacle to safe termination of pregnancy
options.rationale: After 16 weeks, terminations in NSW for serious foetal conditions are accessed in tertiary services with two-doctor approval per the Abortion Law Reform Act 2019; gestational age is not a clinical bar at 21 weeks when the woman has chosen termination. (Source: NSW Health Termination of Pregnancy Policy Directive)
source_refs: NSW Health Termination of Pregnancy Policy Directive

explanation.summary: Second-trimester medical termination in Australia uses mifepristone 200 mg orally followed 24 to 48 hours later by misoprostol 400 micrograms vaginally or buccally every 3 hours until birth. Surgical dilatation and evacuation is an alternative in centres with appropriate expertise and cervical preparation. After 22 weeks in some jurisdictions feticide with intracardiac potassium chloride is considered. Care includes bereavement support, lactation suppression with cabergoline, contraception planning and Rh status review with Anti-D for Rh-negative women.
key_points: Mifepristone priming followed by misoprostol is the Australian second-trimester medical regimen
Surgical D&E requires cervical preparation and trained operators
Feticide with intracardiac potassium chloride is considered from 22 weeks in some jurisdictions
Bereavement support and lactation suppression with cabergoline are routine
Anti-D is given to Rh-negative women undergoing termination at any gestation
explanation.pearls: Cabergoline 1 mg as a single dose given within 24 hours of birth suppresses lactation after second-trimester loss; bromocriptine is no longer used in Australia because of stroke and cardiac risk in this setting.

sources.label: Royal Women's Hospital Termination of Pregnancy Clinical Practice Guideline
NSW Health Termination of Pregnancy Policy Directive

stem: You are the early psychosis service registrar reviewing a 23-year-old non-Aboriginal man 3 months after starting olanzapine 15 mg nightly for a first episode of schizophrenia. Symptoms have improved and he is adherent. He has gained 5 kg since starting treatment. He smokes 10 cigarettes daily, eats takeaway most nights and walks only briefly. His parents and partner are supportive and he is back at his apprenticeship. He has no known cardiovascular disease and no family history of premature heart disease.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 132/82 mmHg, HR 84, RR 14, T 36.7 °C, BMI 27 kg/m2 (up from 25 baseline)
data_table.Examination: Mildly increased waist circumference 96 cm, no acanthosis nigricans, no peripheral oedema, no extrapyramidal signs
data_table.Investigations: Fasting glucose 5.8 mmol/L, HbA1c 5.6%, total cholesterol 5.4 mmol/L, LDL 3.4 mmol/L, HDL 1.0 mmol/L, triglycerides 2.4 mmol/L, prolactin 380 mIU/L (normal up to 500), ECG QTc 432 ms

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Arrange dietitian and exercise physiologist input, schedule repeat fasting lipids and glucose at 6 months, and review olanzapine choice with the prescriber if metabolic indices worsen
options.rationale: RANZCP and Therapeutic Guidelines recommend 3-month then 6-monthly metabolic monitoring for second-generation antipsychotics with intervention via lifestyle and medication review when weight, glucose or lipid changes appear. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia
Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic

options.text: Commence metformin 500 mg twice daily immediately to prevent further weight gain, continue olanzapine 15 mg unchanged, and review the FBC and renal function in 3 months
options.rationale: Metformin is considered after lifestyle measures fail or when significant insulin resistance is present; pre-emptive use in mild weight gain without dysglycaemia is not first-line. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

options.text: Switch immediately to risperidone 4 mg daily without dietitian input, withhold metabolic re-screening for 6 months, and review in psychiatric outpatient clinic in 12 weeks
options.rationale: Switching antipsychotic in stable first-episode response carries relapse risk and is reserved for significant intolerance; risperidone has different but not negligible metabolic and prolactin effects. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

options.text: Refer to cardiology for stress echocardiography given the elevated triglycerides and rising BMI, defer further psychiatry follow-up until that result is available
options.rationale: Stress echocardiography is not indicated in an asymptomatic 23-year-old; cardiovascular risk reduction in early psychosis hinges on lifestyle and medication selection, not advanced imaging. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

options.text: Cease olanzapine, prescribe diazepam 5 mg twice daily for symptom control, and refer back to the general practitioner for ongoing care of the metabolic side effects without psychiatry review
options.rationale: Stopping the antipsychotic in a first-episode patient with response risks relapse; benzodiazepines do not treat psychosis. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

explanation.summary: Second-generation antipsychotics, especially olanzapine and clozapine, cause weight gain, dyslipidaemia and dysglycaemia. RANZCP recommends weight, waist, BP, fasting glucose and lipids at baseline, 6 weeks, 3 months and then 6-monthly. Lifestyle intervention is first-line; metformin can be added when insulin resistance is established or weight gain progresses despite lifestyle. Switching antipsychotics is considered when metabolic harm exceeds psychiatric benefit.
key_points: Olanzapine has high metabolic risk; clozapine highest
Monitor weight, BP, fasting glucose and lipids at baseline, 6 weeks, 3 months, then 6-monthly
Lifestyle intervention is first-line for weight gain and dyslipidaemia
Metformin can be added when insulin resistance is established or weight gain progresses
Switching antipsychotic is considered when metabolic harm outweighs psychiatric benefit
explanation.pearls: Aripiprazole and ziprasidone have the lowest weight gain profile among second-generation antipsychotics and are favoured in first-episode psychosis when metabolic syndrome risk is high or already present.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

subtopic_detail: Cannabis-induced psychotic disorder vs first-episode schizophrenia
stem: You are the consultation-liaison psychiatry registrar called by ED about a 21-year-old non-Aboriginal man brought in by his housemates with 5 days of persecutory delusions and auditory hallucinations. He has used high-potency cannabis daily for 18 months and increased intake markedly over the past month. He has no past psychiatric history and no family history of psychosis. He is calm in the cubicle. Urine drug screen is positive for cannabinoids and negative for amphetamines.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/72 mmHg, HR 88, RR 16, T 36.6 °C, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Alert, oriented to person and time, mildly perplexed, no extrapyramidal signs, no fever, normal cardiovascular and neurological examination
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs, CRP all normal, urine drug screen positive cannabinoids only, ECG QTc 410 ms, CT brain normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Order overnight observation in ED with diazepam 5 mg twice daily for sedation only, withhold antipsychotic and AOD counselling, and discharge in the morning to GP follow-up
options.rationale: Benzodiazepine monotherapy does not treat psychotic symptoms; same-day discharge from ED without an antipsychotic plan or specialist follow-up misses the early-intervention window. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

options.text: Discharge home with instructions to cease cannabis, withhold antipsychotic treatment, and review in early psychosis clinic in 2 weeks once a baseline assessment is complete
options.rationale: Active psychotic symptoms are unsafe to manage at home and warrant antipsychotic treatment; abstinence alone often does not resolve symptoms quickly. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

options.text: Detain under the Mental Health Act for compulsory inpatient treatment and commence intramuscular haloperidol 5 mg every 6 hours for symptom control on admission
options.rationale: Compulsory detention is reserved for refusal of treatment with risk; he is calm and agreeable. Haloperidol intramuscular every 6 hours is overaggressive and risks extrapyramidal side effects. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

options.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg daily for the perplexity and persecutory thoughts, recommend cannabis cessation, and arrange GP review in 1 week for medication titration
options.rationale: SSRIs do not treat psychosis and can worsen symptoms; antipsychotic is required. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

options.text: Admit voluntarily, commence olanzapine 10 mg nightly, arrange brief alcohol and other drug counselling, and plan psychiatric review in 4 weeks with abstinence advice
options.rationale: First-presentation psychosis in the context of heavy cannabis use needs antipsychotic treatment, abstinence support, and structured follow-up because around half of cannabis-induced psychoses transition to a primary psychotic disorder. (Source: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia)
source_refs: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia
Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic

explanation.summary: Cannabis-induced psychotic disorder and first-episode schizophrenia overlap significantly and are not reliably distinguishable at first presentation. Around half of cannabis-induced psychoses transition to a primary psychotic disorder within 3 years, so management mirrors first-episode psychosis with antipsychotic treatment, AOD intervention and structured follow-up. High-potency cannabis use confers the greatest risk. Voluntary admission is preferred when engagement allows.
key_points: Cannabis-induced and primary psychotic disorders overlap clinically at first presentation
Around 50% of cannabis-induced psychoses transition to primary psychotic disorder within 3 years
Treat with antipsychotic plus abstinence support and structured follow-up
High-potency cannabis use confers the greatest risk
Voluntary admission is preferred when the patient is engaging and safe
explanation.pearls: Daily use of high-potency cannabis (over 10% THC content) is associated with a nearly 5-fold increased risk of psychotic disorder compared with non-users; risk is highest under 18 years of age.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizophrenia

stem: You are the consultation-liaison psychiatry registrar called to the medical ward to see a 28-year-old non-Aboriginal woman with bipolar I disorder admitted 4 days ago for refusing food and fluids. She has been mute, immobile and maintaining odd postures. She has not eaten or drunk anything. She has not had recent neuroleptic dose change. Medical causes have been excluded by neurology consult. She is on lithium 750 mg nightly.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.0 °C, HR 96, RR 18, BP 116/72 mmHg, SpO2 98% room air
data_table.Examination: Mute, eyes open without engagement, waxy flexibility with arms remaining where placed, mild echopraxia of examiner gestures, Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale score 14, no rigidity or pyrexia
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs normal, lithium level 0.72 mmol/L, CK 320 U/L, CRP 4 mg/L, CT brain normal, EEG without epileptiform activity

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence intramuscular haloperidol 5 mg twice daily for the immobility and mutism, observe for further extrapyramidal features, and review the response after 48 hours
options.rationale: Antipsychotics can precipitate malignant catatonia and worsen the syndrome; they are avoided in suspected catatonia until benzodiazepines are first trialled. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Clinical Memorandum)
source_refs: RANZCP Catatonia Clinical Memorandum

options.text: Administer a lorazepam challenge with 1 mg intramuscularly and reassess at 30 minutes, planning ongoing scheduled lorazepam if responsive and ECT referral if not
options.rationale: The lorazepam challenge is the standard diagnostic and therapeutic test for catatonia; a marked reduction in catatonic features within 30 minutes confirms the diagnosis and supports continued benzodiazepine treatment, with ECT for non-response. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Clinical Memorandum)
source_refs: RANZCP Catatonia Clinical Memorandum
Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic

options.text: Increase the lithium dose to 1000 mg nightly, recheck the level in 5 days, and continue ward observation pending mood stabiliser optimisation over the next week
options.rationale: Lithium augmentation is not the treatment for catatonia and the level is already therapeutic; a benzodiazepine challenge addresses the immediate problem. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Clinical Memorandum)
source_refs: RANZCP Catatonia Clinical Memorandum

options.text: Commence intravenous valproate loading at 20 mg/kg in addition to lithium, withhold benzodiazepines, and reassess catatonic features after 4 hours of observation
options.rationale: Valproate is not first-line for catatonia and combined valproate-lithium does not address the syndrome; benzodiazepines plus ECT are the evidence-based path. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Clinical Memorandum)
source_refs: RANZCP Catatonia Clinical Memorandum

options.text: Refer for nasogastric tube insertion for feeds and rehydration, withhold psychotropic intervention until oral intake resumes, and review with psychiatry team in 24 hours
options.rationale: NG feeding may be needed but does not treat catatonia; deferring psychotropic intervention prolongs the syndrome and risks complications such as pulmonary embolism and contractures. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Clinical Memorandum)
source_refs: RANZCP Catatonia Clinical Memorandum

explanation.summary: Catatonia is a neuropsychiatric syndrome of stupor, mutism, posturing, waxy flexibility and echophenomena, frequently associated with mood disorders, psychotic illness and medical conditions. The lorazepam challenge with 1 to 2 mg IM or IV provides rapid diagnostic confirmation and therapeutic effect; responders continue scheduled lorazepam. Non-responders or those with malignant features (autonomic instability, hyperthermia) need urgent ECT. Antipsychotics are avoided until catatonia resolves.
key_points: Catatonia features include stupor, mutism, posturing, waxy flexibility and echophenomena
Lorazepam challenge is the diagnostic and therapeutic standard
Scheduled lorazepam continues for responders; ECT for non-response or malignant features
Avoid antipsychotics until catatonia resolves due to malignant catatonia risk
Bush-Francis Catatonia Rating Scale aids diagnosis and severity tracking
explanation.pearls: Malignant catatonia (autonomic instability, hyperthermia, severe rigidity) and neuroleptic malignant syndrome are increasingly viewed as variants of the same neurochemical spectrum; ECT is effective for both and should not be delayed in critically ill patients.

subtopic_detail: Valproate teratogenicity and pregnancy prevention in a woman of reproductive age
stem: You are the psychiatry registrar in the mood disorders clinic reviewing a 27-year-old non-Aboriginal woman with bipolar I disorder who has been stable for 18 months on sodium valproate 1000 mg nightly. She is in a new relationship and has stopped using contraception because she wants to start trying for a baby in the next 6 months. She has had no episodes for 18 months and is otherwise well. She works as a primary school teacher and has supportive parents nearby. There is no other medical or psychiatric history.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/76 mmHg, HR 76, RR 14, T 36.6 °C, BMI 24 kg/m2
data_table.Examination: Euthymic mental state, normal neurological examination, no signs of polycystic ovary syndrome, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal, valproate level 480 micromol/L (therapeutic 350 to 700), LFTs normal, urine pregnancy test negative, TSH 1.6 mIU/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Switch to carbamazepine 400 mg daily as an alternative mood stabiliser with the rationale that it has a more favourable foetal safety profile in pregnancy planning settings
options.rationale: Carbamazepine has higher major malformation rates (around 5%) than lamotrigine (around 2%) and is not the preferred switch for pregnancy planning in bipolar disorder. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: RANZCP Bipolar Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Continue valproate at the current dose, add folic acid 5 mg daily, and reassure that the medication is safer for the baby than a relapse during pregnancy in this stable patient
options.rationale: Folate does not mitigate valproate teratogenicity or neurodevelopmental risk; continuation in pregnancy planning is contraindicated outside extraordinary circumstances. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Advice)
source_refs: Therapeutic Goods Administration Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Advice

options.text: Counsel that valproate is contraindicated in pregnancy planning, arrange specialist review to transition to a safer mood stabiliser such as lamotrigine, and continue effective contraception during the switch
options.rationale: Valproate carries 10% major congenital malformation risk and a 30 to 40% risk of neurodevelopmental impairment; women of reproductive potential planning pregnancy require transition to safer agents under specialist supervision with continued contraception. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: RANZCP Bipolar Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline
Therapeutic Goods Administration Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Advice

options.text: Cease valproate immediately without replacement therapy, commence cognitive behavioural therapy fortnightly, and review for relapse with monthly mood diaries and clinic visits
options.rationale: Abrupt valproate cessation in bipolar I without bridging risks severe relapse; transition under specialist guidance to a maintenance-effective alternative is required. (Source: RANZCP Bipolar Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline)
source_refs: RANZCP Bipolar Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline

options.text: Continue valproate during the periconceptional period only, switch to lamotrigine after the first trimester, and arrange detailed morphology scan at 20 weeks
options.rationale: Neural tube defect risk is highest in the first trimester so continuing valproate during that window is exactly the wrong sequence; safer agents must be in place before conception. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Advice)
source_refs: Therapeutic Goods Administration Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Advice

explanation.summary: Valproate causes major congenital malformations in around 10% of exposed pregnancies and neurodevelopmental impairment in 30 to 40%, particularly autism spectrum and reduced IQ. The TGA pregnancy prevention program requires annual specialist review, documented contraception and a signed acknowledgement for women of reproductive potential. Pregnancy planning requires transition to safer agents such as lamotrigine under specialist supervision before conception. Folate does not mitigate teratogenicity.
key_points: Valproate causes around 10% major malformations and 30 to 40% neurodevelopmental impairment
TGA pregnancy prevention program mandates annual review and documented contraception
Transition to safer agents such as lamotrigine occurs before conception, not during pregnancy
Folate does not mitigate valproate teratogenicity
Lamotrigine has the lowest teratogenic risk of the mood stabilisers (around 2% major malformation)
explanation.pearls: Lamotrigine clearance increases by up to 250% during pregnancy due to glucuronidation induction; doses typically need to rise from second trimester and return to baseline within 2 weeks postpartum to avoid toxicity.

sources.label: RANZCP Bipolar Disorder Clinical Practice Guideline
Therapeutic Goods Administration Valproate Pregnancy Prevention Advice
